museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: The year opens inside the arcade boom: Ms. Pac-Man and Zaxxon become cabinet names while the Commodore 64 is introduced to the trade and Britain waits for cheaper colour micros.
museum.curatorNote: January 1982 is strong on arcade and hardware signals, weaker on exact UK street dates. Items marked as context are included as shelf or anticipation objects, not false day-one claims.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: Five signals from a year beginning at full arcade volume.

items.title: Ms. Pac-Man enters the copyright record
items.body: Midway's sequel/spin-off to Pac-Man is tied by US copyright records to 13 January 1982, though contemporary availability is sometimes described as late January or early February.
items.sourceNote: US copyright date cited by Pac-Man history references and Neon Rocketship; Pac-Man Wiki notes late-January/early-February ambiguity.

items.title: Zaxxon makes isometric space feel new
items.body: Sega's isometric shooter appears in Japanese arcade records in January, giving 1982 one of its strongest visual signatures.
items.sourceNote: VGMdb and GameFAQs list Zaxxon Japan arcade release as January 1982.

items.title: Commodore introduces the C64 at CES
items.body: The Commodore 64 was introduced at the January Consumer Electronics Show, months before its wider marketing and retail push.
items.sourceNote: Commodore 64 histories list introduction at January 1982 CES and marketing from August.

items.title: Britain's low-cost colour micro moment approaches
items.body: The ZX Spectrum was not out yet, but UK readers were already living in a world where home computing was moving from hobbyist luxury toward bedroom possibility.
items.sourceNote: ZX Spectrum launched in April 1982; this is pre-launch UK context.

items.title: Pac-Man remains the gravity well
items.body: Before the Atari 2600 port even reached shops, Pac-Man still organised arcade talk, home-console desire and magazine attention.
items.sourceNote: Atari 2600 Pac-Man followed in March/April 1982; arcade Pac-Man remained a top-grossing 1982 title.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight shelf objects and release-window anchors, with cautious dating where records blur.

items.title: Ms. Pac-Man
items.body: The maze sequel becomes one of the defining cabinets of the year.
items.certainty: publication date verified; operator rollout fuzzy
items.sourceNote: See news note.

items.title: Zaxxon
items.body: A pseudo-3D shooter whose angled perspective made it look impossible at a glance.
items.sourceNote: VGMdb/GameFAQs Japan arcade listings.

items.title: Pac-Man anticipation
items.body: Atari's home Pac-Man was imminent, already sold as an event before players saw the compromise.
items.sourceNote: Atari 2600 Pac-Man released in March with an April 3 promotional Pac-Man Day.

items.title: Donkey Kong
items.body: Nintendo's 1981 hit was still shaping what players expected from character-led arcade games.
items.sourceNote: Not a January 1982 release; included as active arcade context.

items.title: Frogger
items.body: Konami/Sega's road-and-river game remained a key family-friendly arcade object around the turn of 1982.
items.sourceNote: Release timing predates 1982 in many regions; included as shelf context.

items.title: Defender
items.body: Williams' 1981 shooter still defined difficulty and control density for arcade players entering 1982.
items.sourceNote: Not a January 1982 release.

items.title: Commodore 64 demos
items.body: Not a retail game, but a trade-show software promise: sprites, sound, and colour aimed at the living room.
items.sourceNote: C64 introduced at January CES.

items.title: ZX Spectrum software promise
items.body: The games were not on the shelf yet, but the British micro market was about to shift toward cheap cassette software.
items.sourceNote: ZX Spectrum launch followed in April.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: The month is a hardware forecast: trade-show power, arcade cabinets and British micro dreams.

items.title: Commodore 64 introduction
items.body: The C64's sound and sprite hardware promised a more game-friendly home computer before most buyers could actually bring one home.
items.sourceNote: C64 histories list January 1982 CES introduction.

items.title: Atari VCS / 2600 dominance
items.body: Atari's console still owned the home game conversation, even as pressure from third parties and new hardware grew.
items.sourceNote: Atari 2600 installed-base and Pac-Man context.

items.title: Coin-op cabinet as premium display
items.body: Arcade games were still the most powerful public hardware most players touched.
items.sourceNote: Arcade-boom context.

items.title: UK micros before the Spectrum
items.body: ZX81, VIC-20 and BBC Micro owners already knew games could arrive as listings, cassettes and typed-in experiments.
items.sourceNote: UK home-micro context before the April Spectrum launch.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: The magazine shelf was the early-1982 network.

items.title: Electronic Games
items.headline: Arcade boom reporting
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Electronic Games style cover for January 1982.
items.curatorNote: American arcade coverage made cabinets feel like a national culture rather than scattered machines.
items.body: American arcade coverage made cabinets feel like a national culture rather than scattered machines.
items.sourceNote: Specific January cover line not asserted.

items.title: Computer and Video Games
items.headline: UK arcade and micro coverage
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed C&VG style cover for January 1982.
items.curatorNote: C&VG gave UK readers a bridge between coin-op spectacle and bedroom computing.
items.body: C&VG gave UK readers a bridge between coin-op spectacle and bedroom computing.
items.sourceNote: Specific January cover line not asserted.

items.title: Personal Computer World
items.headline: Home computing as serious hobby
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Personal Computer World style cover for January 1982.
items.curatorNote: Not primarily a games magazine, but essential to the UK environment that made microcomputer games possible.
items.body: Not primarily a games magazine, but essential to the UK environment that made microcomputer games possible.
items.sourceNote: Specific January issue line not asserted.

items.title: Popular Computing Weekly
items.headline: Listings, adverts and weekly machine gossip
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Popular Computing Weekly style cover for January 1982.
items.curatorNote: Weekly computing papers made software feel closer and more immediate than monthly magazines.
items.body: Weekly computing papers made software feel closer and more immediate than monthly magazines.
items.sourceNote: Specific January issue line not asserted.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: For almost everyone, discovery was physical.

items.title: The arcade was the feed
items.body: New games were discovered by walking into a space and seeing a different cabinet lit up.

items.title: Magazine listings were software delivery
items.body: Typed code and printed adverts were a slow, paper network.

items.title: Mail order mattered
items.body: Small software sellers and hardware suppliers reached players through forms and postal patience.

items.title: Networks were specialist, not ordinary
items.body: Bulletin boards existed, but not as the default gaming life of a UK player in January 1982.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: January 1982 felt like a boom before anyone called it fragile.

items.title: Arcades looked richer than home
items.body: The cabinet was still the full-colour future.

items.title: Home machines felt imminent
items.body: A computer at home was becoming imaginable, not yet normal.

items.title: Characters were getting names
items.body: Ms. Pac-Man and Q*bert made arcade games feel like personalities.

items.title: The year sounded busy already
items.body: Even in January, 1982 felt packed with machines about to arrive.
